section.title: Ephraim and Manasseh
section.sectionRationale: The two tribes of Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, receive their inheritance in the central highlands. The daughters of Zelophehad rightly claim their portion by faith in God's command; but both tribes fail to drive out the Canaanites, and then the house of Joseph complains that their allotment is too small, only to be told by Joshua to trust God and clear and conquer the land they already have. It is one movement, the inheritance of Joseph, contrasting believing initiative with faithless complaint.
section.thesis: God gives Joseph an ample inheritance, and the daughters of Zelophehad show faith that lays hold of it, but the tribes' failure to drive out the Canaanites and their grumbling about too little land expose a faithlessness that shrinks back from the very conquest God had promised to bless, so that the fault lies not in God's gift but in their unwillingness to trust and act.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Ephraim's borders and its failure at Gezer, 16:1 to 10; Manasseh's allotment and the faith of Zelophehad's daughters, 17:1 to 6; Manasseh's territory and failure to drive out the Canaanites, 17:7 to 13; and the house of Joseph's complaint answered by Joshua's challenge, 17:14 to 18.

units.label: Ephraim's borders and failure at Gezer
units.note: they did not drive out the Canaanites in Gezer

verses.text: The allotment for the descendants of Joseph extended from the Jordan at Jericho to the waters of Jericho on the east, through the wilderness that goes up from Jericho into the hill country of Bethel.
It went on from Bethel (that is, Luz) and proceeded to the border of the Archites in Ataroth.
Then it descended westward to the border of the Japhletites as far as the border of Lower Beth-horon and on to Gezer, and it ended at the Sea.
So Ephraim and Manasseh, the sons of Joseph, received their {{nachalah|inheritance}}.
This was the territory of the descendants of Ephraim by their clans: The border of their inheritance went from Ataroth-addar in the east to Upper Beth-horon
and out toward the Sea. From Michmethath on the north it turned eastward toward Taanath-shiloh and passed by it to Janoah on the east.
From Janoah it went down to Ataroth and Naarah, and then reached Jericho and came out at the Jordan.
From Tappuah the border went westward to the Brook of Kanah and ended at the Sea. This was the inheritance of the clans of the tribe of Ephraim,
along with all the cities and villages set apart for the descendants of Ephraim within the inheritance of Manasseh.
But they did not drive out the Canaanites who lived in Gezer. So the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites to this day, but they are forced laborers.

units.label: Manasseh's allotment and the daughters of Zelophehad
units.note: the LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance

verses.text: Now this was the allotment for the tribe of Manasseh as Joseph’s firstborn son, namely for Machir the firstborn of Manasseh and father of the Gileadites, who had received Gilead and Bashan because Machir was a man of war.

So this allotment was for the rest of the descendants of Manasseh, the clans of Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, and Shemida. These are the other male descendants of the clans of Manasseh son of Joseph.

But Zelophehad son of Hepher (the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh) had no sons but only daughters. These are the names of his daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

They approached Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the leaders, and said, “The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brothers.” So Joshua gave them an inheritance among their father’s brothers, in keeping with the command of the LORD.
Thus ten shares fell to Manasseh, in addition to the land of Gilead and Bashan beyond the Jordan,
because the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance among his sons. And the land of Gilead belonged to the rest of the sons of Manasseh.

units.label: Manasseh's territory and failure to drive out the Canaanites
units.note: the Canaanites were determined to stay in the land

verses.text: Now the border of Manasseh went from Asher to Michmethath near Shechem, then southward to include the inhabitants of En-tappuah.
The region of Tappuah belonged to Manasseh, but Tappuah itself, on the border of Manasseh, belonged to Ephraim.

From there the border continued southward to the Brook of Kanah. There were cities belonging to Ephraim among the cities of Manasseh, but the border of Manasseh was on the north side of the brook and ended at the Sea.
Ephraim’s territory was to the south, and Manasseh’s was to the north, having the Sea as its border and adjoining Asher on the north and Issachar on the east.
Within Issachar and Asher, Manasseh was assigned Beth-shean, Ibleam, Dor (that is, Naphath), Endor, Taanach, and Megiddo, each with their surrounding settlements.
But the descendants of Manasseh were unable to occupy these cities, because the Canaanites were determined to stay in this land.
However, when the Israelites grew {{chazaq|stronger}}, they put the Canaanites to forced labor; but they failed to drive them out completely.

units.label: The house of Joseph's complaint and Joshua's challenge

verses.text: Then the sons of Joseph said to Joshua, “Why have you given us only one portion as an inheritance? We have many people, because the LORD has blessed us abundantly.”

Joshua answered them, “If you have so many people that the hill country of Ephraim is too small for you, go to the forest and clear for yourself an area in the land of the Perizzites and the Rephaim.”
“The hill country is not enough for us,” they replied, “and all the Canaanites who live in the valley have iron chariots, both in Beth-shean with its towns and in the Valley of Jezreel.”
So Joshua said to the house of Joseph, to Ephraim and Manasseh, “You have many people and great strength. You shall not have just one allotment,

because the hill country will be yours as well. It is a forest; clear it, and its farthest limits will be yours. Although the Canaanites have iron chariots and although they are strong, you can {{yarash|drive}} them out.”

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. The sons of Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, receive their nachalah in the fertile central highlands. The daughters of Zelophehad rightly claim a nachalah among their brothers because the LORD had commanded it through Moses, showing that God's provision of inheritance reached even to daughters where there were no sons, honoring His own word.

chazaq.gloss: To be strong, become strong. When Israel grew chazaq, they subjected the Canaanites to forced labor but still did not drive them out. The word is poignant: the very strength that could have completed the conquest was used only to exploit the Canaanites rather than to obey God fully, a compromise of half-obedience born of a lack of resolve to trust God.

yarash.gloss: To take possession of, dispossess, drive out. Joshua insists, despite the Canaanites' iron chariots, you can drive them out. The failure of Joseph to yarash the land was not because God's promise was too small or the enemy too strong, but because their faith faltered; Joshua's charge presses them to trust the God who had promised the land and act on it.

basic.context: The two tribes descended from Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, receive their land in the rich central highlands. A bright spot comes in the daughters of Zelophehad: their father had died with no sons, and they had earlier asked Moses for their family's inheritance; now, on the basis of God's command, they receive it. But a shadow falls too. Ephraim fails to drive the Canaanites out of Gezer, and Manasseh cannot take its assigned cities because the Canaanites are determined to stay; when Israel grows strong, they merely enslave them rather than obey God fully. Then the house of Joseph complains to Joshua that their inheritance is too small for such a large, blessed people. Joshua replies bluntly: you have the strength and the promise, so go clear the forest and drive out the Canaanites, iron chariots and all, you can do it. The question underneath is: is the problem God's provision, or the people's faith?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, mixing land records with two revealing episodes. The daughters of Zelophehad model faith that lays hold of God's promise. The repeated failure to drive out the Canaanites, and the complaint of Joseph, expose faithlessness, and the narrative subtly indicts them: the fault is not in God's gift but in their unwillingness to trust and act. We should read the failures as warnings, not models.
basic.originalLanguages: Joseph's tribes receive their nachalah, inheritance, and the daughters of Zelophehad claim their nachalah by God's command. Yet when Israel grew chazaq, strong, they only enslaved the Canaanites; and Joshua insists they can yarash, drive out, the enemy despite the chariots, if they will trust God.
basic.typology: The daughters of Zelophehad, claiming their inheritance by faith in God's word, picture all who lay hold of the promised inheritance through faith. The failure of Joseph to drive out the enemy warns against the unbelief that forfeits what God has given, a warning the New Testament applies to those who shrink back.
basic.christ: The contrast between believing faith and faithless complaint points to the necessity of trusting God's promise, fulfilled in Christ, in whom all God's promises are Yes. Where Israel shrank back from the inheritance in unbelief, Christ secures the inheritance for His people and calls them to enter it by faith, not to draw back to destruction.
basic.god: God is generous, giving Joseph an ample inheritance and blessing them abundantly. He is faithful to His own word, honoring the daughters' claim because He had commanded it. And He is the God who promises victory even over iron chariots, so that the failure to possess the land indicts the people's faith, not His faithfulness.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 27 records the daughters of Zelophehad first securing their inheritance. Judges 1 details Israel's failure to drive out the Canaanites. Hebrews 10 warns against shrinking back in unbelief. And 2 Corinthians 1 says all God's promises find their Yes in Christ.
basic.oneStory: Joseph's tribes receive a generous inheritance, and the daughters of Zelophehad show faith that claims God's promise, but the tribes' failure to drive out the Canaanites and their grumbling reveal a faithless shrinking back. The contrast presses the lesson of the whole book: the inheritance is God's sure gift, entered only by faith, a faith perfected in Christ.

context.literary: This section covers the inheritance of the Joseph tribes, structured as boundary descriptions punctuated by two telling narratives: the faith of Zelophehad's daughters and the complaint of the house of Joseph. Both Ephraim and Manasseh's accounts end with the same refrain of failure to drive out the Canaanites, tying them together and continuing the pattern begun with Judah in 15:63. The closing dialogue between Joseph and Joshua is pointed, exposing the tribes' excuses and calling them to faith, and it echoes the courage-language that runs through the book.
context.historical: Ephraim and Manasseh were the two half-tribes descended from Joseph's sons, counted separately so that Joseph effectively received a double portion, the birthright forfeited by Reuben, 1 Chronicles 5:1-2. Their land lay in the fertile central hill country. Zelophehad's daughters had won a landmark ruling under Moses that daughters could inherit where there were no sons, Numbers 27; their claim here shows that ruling honored. Gezer, Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, and the Jezreel Valley were strategic sites where Canaanite forces with iron chariots held the lowlands; iron chariots gave a decisive military edge on flat ground, which the tribes used as an excuse. The forested hill country could be cleared and settled with effort.
context.canonical: The section develops the theme of inheritance received by faith, embodied positively in Zelophehad's daughters and negatively in Joseph's complaint. The recurring failure to drive out the Canaanites, honestly recorded through these chapters, sets up the tragic pattern of Judges, where the un-driven-out nations become snares to Israel, Judges 2:1-3. Joshua's rebuke, you can drive them out, anticipates the New Testament call to enter God's promises by faith and not to shrink back, Hebrews 10:38-39, so that the episode warns every generation against forfeiting God's gifts through unbelief.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the inheritance of the Joseph tribes, to commend the faith of Zelophehad's daughters who claimed their portion on God's command, and to expose the faithlessness of the tribes who failed to drive out the Canaanites and complained about their inheritance, showing that the fault lay in their unbelief, not in God's provision.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The land records describe historical settlement. The daughters' faith is held up positively; the tribes' failure and complaint are recorded as a warning, not a model. What is prescriptive is the lesson the narrative drives home: God's promised inheritance is ample and sure, and it must be laid hold of by faith and obedient action rather than forfeited through unbelief and excuse-making.

debates.question: Was the house of Joseph's complaint about too little land legitimate?

views.label: No; it masked unbelief with a grievance
views.case: Joshua's reply exposes the real issue: they had many people and great strength and were told to go clear the forest and drive out the Canaanites, yet they balked at the iron chariots; their complaint about size was an excuse for unwillingness to trust God and do the hard work of possessing the land He had given, so the fault was faith, not acreage.

views.label: Partly practical, but still a failure of faith
views.case: The Jezreel valley Canaanites with iron chariots were a genuine military challenge, so the concern was not baseless; yet God had repeatedly promised victory even over chariots, Joshua 11, so to let the chariots become a reason for inaction was to distrust the God who fights for His people, turning a real difficulty into an excuse.

debates.bearing: Either way, Joshua treats the complaint as a summons to faith: the inheritance is ample and the enemy conquerable with God's help, so the tribes are called to trust and act rather than grumble, a lesson against letting difficulties become excuses for unbelief.

study.originalLanguages: The section turns on the word nachalah, inheritance, and the contrast between two ways of relating to it. Positively, the daughters of Zelophehad come and claim their nachalah among their brothers, resting their claim on the fact that the LORD commanded Moses; their faith simply takes God at His word, and Joshua grants it, in keeping with the command of the LORD. Negatively, the tribes fail to lay hold of what God gave. When Israel grew chazaq, strong, the same word used for the repeated command be strong, they used that strength not to obey God fully but merely to press the Canaanites into forced labor, a half-obedience that stopped short of driving them out. And when Joseph complains, Joshua flatly rejects the excuse of the iron chariots: you can yarash, drive them out. The verb of conquest is put back in their hands as a promise and a summons; the enemy's chariots do not cancel God's pledge. Inheritance claimed by faith, strength misused for compromise, a driving-out left undone through unbelief: the vocabulary sets believing initiative against faithless excuse.

typology.type: The daughters of Zelophehad claiming their inheritance by faith in God's word
typology.fulfillment: All who lay hold of the promised inheritance through faith, resting on God's sure command and promise, Numbers 27:6-7 and Galatians 3:29.

typology.type: Joseph shrinking back from the inheritance in unbelief
typology.fulfillment: A warning against those who shrink back and so fail to enter God's promises, and the call to persevere by faith, Hebrews 10:38-39 and 3:19.

typology.type: Joshua's charge, you can drive them out, despite the chariots
typology.fulfillment: The call to trust God's promise over visible obstacles, since with God nothing is impossible, Luke 1:37 and Romans 8:31.

study.christ: This section sets faith and unbelief side by side, and it drives us to Christ, in whom every promise of God is Yes and Amen, 2 Corinthians 1:20. The daughters of Zelophehad show the shape of saving faith: they simply take God at His word and lay hold of the inheritance He promised, a picture of all who, in Christ, are heirs according to promise, Galatians 3:29. The tribes of Joseph show the opposite: given ample land and great strength, they let the iron chariots become an excuse and shrank back from possessing what God had given. Hebrews takes exactly that danger and warns, my righteous one shall live by faith, and if he shrinks back, my soul has no pleasure in him; but we are not of those who shrink back and are destroyed, but of those who have faith and preserve their souls, Hebrews 10:38-39. Where Israel forfeited what God gave through unbelief, Christ secures the inheritance for His people and calls them to enter it by faith, trusting the God who promises victory even over what looks unconquerable.
study.god: The God of this section is generous, faithful, and mighty, and the failures throw His goodness into relief. He is generous, giving Joseph a large and fertile inheritance and, as they themselves admit, blessing them abundantly. He is faithful to His own word, honoring the claim of Zelophehad's daughters precisely because He had commanded it through Moses; His promises reach even to daughters without brothers, and He keeps them. And He is mighty to give victory, pledging through Joshua that the tribes can drive out the Canaanites despite their iron chariots. So when the tribes fail to possess the land and grumble that it is too small, the indictment falls on their unbelief, not on God's provision. The God who gives the inheritance is entirely trustworthy; the tragedy is a people who will not trust and act on what He has so freely given.

crossReferences.note: The daughters of Zelophehad first secure the right to inherit; their faith-claim here rests on that command of the LORD.
The failure of Manasseh and Ephraim to drive out the Canaanites is detailed, showing the consequences of this incomplete obedience.
God had already given victory over chariots at Merom, so the iron chariots were no true obstacle to faith.
The righteous live by faith and do not shrink back to destruction, the warning Joseph's failure illustrates.
All the promises of God find their Yes in Christ, the ground of the faith Israel failed to exercise.

study.oneStory: The tribes of Joseph receive a generous inheritance, and the daughters of Zelophehad model faith that claims God's promise, but the tribes' failure to drive out the Canaanites and their grumbling about too little land expose a faithless shrinking back. The contrast presses the central lesson of Joshua: the inheritance is God's sure and ample gift, entered only by faith and obedient action. That faith is perfected in Christ, in whom every promise is Yes, and who secures the inheritance for all who will not shrink back but trust Him.

quiz.q: Why did the daughters of Zelophehad receive an inheritance among their brothers?

options.text: Because the LORD had commanded Moses that they should, and they claimed it by faith in His word.
options.note: Joshua 17:3 to 4 grants their inheritance in keeping with the command of the LORD.

options.text: Because they bought the land from the other clans.
options.note: No purchase was involved; their inheritance rested on God's command, not payment.

options.text: Because their father had been the strongest warrior.
options.note: Their father Zelophehad had no sons; their claim rested on God's command, not their father's valor.
Joshua acted in keeping with the LORD's command, not personal favoritism.

quiz.q: What repeatedly happened when the Joseph tribes met the Canaanites in their land?

options.text: They failed to drive them out completely, at most putting them to forced labor.
options.note: Joshua 16:10 and 17:12 to 13 record the incomplete obedience of both tribes.

options.text: They drove out every Canaanite thoroughly.
options.note: The opposite: they failed to drive them out; the Canaanites remained among them.

options.text: They made peace treaties with all of them.
options.note: There were no treaties; the tribes simply failed to complete the conquest.

options.text: They were completely defeated and driven out themselves.
options.note: The tribes held their land but failed to drive out the Canaanites; they were not driven out themselves.

quiz.q: How did Joshua answer the house of Joseph's complaint that their land was too small?

options.text: He told them they had the strength and the promise to clear the forest and drive out the Canaanites, chariots and all.
options.note: Joshua 17:17 to 18 turns their complaint into a call to faith and action.

options.text: He immediately gave them another tribe's land.
options.note: He did not reassign another tribe's land; he challenged them to possess and clear what they had.

options.text: He agreed the land was too small and apologized.
options.note: He did not concede the point; he exposed their unbelief and called them to act.

options.text: He told them to give up and settle for less.
options.note: The opposite: he charged them to take more by clearing the forest and driving out the enemy.

quiz.q: What was the real problem behind the tribes' failure and complaint?

options.text: Their unbelief and unwillingness to trust and act on God's promise, not any lack in God's provision.
options.note: Joshua's rebuke shows the inheritance was ample and the enemy conquerable; the failure was faith.

options.text: God had genuinely given them too little land.
options.note: Joshua shows the land was ample and could be enlarged; the problem was their faith, not God's gift.

options.text: The Canaanites were simply impossible to defeat.
options.note: God had already given victory over chariots at Merom; the enemy was conquerable with God's help.

options.text: Joshua had unfairly favored the other tribes.
options.note: Joshua challenged them to possess what God had given; the issue was their unbelief, not favoritism.

questions: The daughters of Zelophehad boldly claimed what God had promised. Where do you need to take God at His word and lay hold of a promise rather than passively waiting?
When Israel grew strong, they used their strength for compromise rather than full obedience. Are there areas where you settle for managing a sin or problem instead of dealing with it fully?
The tribes let the iron chariots become an excuse for inaction. What obstacle are you letting become a reason not to obey or trust God?

Joseph complained that their inheritance was too small, when really they had not exerted the faith to possess it. Where are you tempted to grumble about your circumstances rather than trust God and act?
Joshua said, you can drive them out. How does hearing God's you can, grounded in His promise, change the way you face a hard, seemingly impossible task this week?

translationNotes.note: Manasseh is called Joseph's firstborn, and the two Joseph tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, together receive a double portion, the birthright forfeited by Reuben, 1 Chronicles 5:1-2.
The BSB sets off the list of clans with an em dash; this has been rendered as a comma here, with no change to the wording.
The cities of the valley, including Beth-shean, Taanach, and Megiddo, were strategic Canaanite strongholds with iron chariots, which the tribes failed to take.
The BSB sets off to Ephraim and Manasseh with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 16:1-10 and 17:1-18, the movement spanning both chapters. Line-break markers within verses were ignored and segments joined with single spaces. The em dashes in 17:2 and 17:17 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
